finding.recommendation: Use 9 ordered phase children: parity baseline plus runtime ownership ADR, runtime backend promotions, merged workflow hub and mode packets, command repoint, agent mirror repoint, advisor graph mode-routing collapse, governance consolidation, framework docs sweep, and old-skill deletion with full-surface validation. This sequencing keeps command artifacts byte-identical per mode after each behavior-touching phase, avoids advisor collapse before dispatch paths are live, keeps governance path rewrites after layout stabilization, and reserves deletion of the five old skill dirs until every command, agent, advisor, governance, docs, and runtime validation gate is green.
key_tradeoffs: Nine phases are heavier than a 5-6 phase plan but isolate the high-risk surfaces: runtime ownership, command YAML paths, agent mirrors, advisor graph identity, governance source tables, docs, and destructive deletion.
The merged skill can be staged before advisor collapse, accepting a temporary duplicate physical surface to preserve rollback and parity while direct dispatch is repointed.
Use direct mode packets under deep-loop-workflows rather than modes/<mode> to minimize path churn, with ai-council as the packet folder and council as a router/advisor alias.
risks: Runtime ownership ADR is mandatory before promoting backend helpers for the new merged consumer.
Advisor alias collapse can hide mode regressions unless mode-discriminator fixtures stay distinct.
Governance SOURCE-FILES rewrites are the largest mechanical surface and can silently rot if run before final path layout.
Lane D must remain adapter-backed; changing argv/env/journal/kill-switch semantics would trigger external contract coordination.
Deleting old skill dirs before graph/advisor/docs/path greps are clean can create hard-to-diagnose UNKNOWN-TARGET and broken path failures.
depends_on: Packet 123 phase 006 byte-identical single-executor parity precedent
Packet 143 phase 002 Lane D adapter and agent-surface precedent
